text: Sometimes over the weekend, the market
will gap lower or higher, and the
opening price on Monday will be far away
from where it was when the market closed
on Sunday. This is actually a good thing
because market gaps can give you some
incredibly easy, simple, and
straightforward trades, which if
executed properly, can actually set you
up with all of the profits you would
need for a good week. In this video,
we're going to go through what market
gaps are, where they form, and I'm going
to show you exactly how to trade them.
Two different types of trades,
rebalancing trades and sentiment trades.
Let's get to it. So, what is a gap?
Well, a gap is simply a price jump,
generally over the weekend or through a
market break, where demand has either
increased or decreased, driving the
market to jump higher or sometimes jump
lower dependent on whether demand is now
higher or lower than it was at the
previous close. So, a gap can
essentially be seen as just a large
candle. You can envision a large candle
leaving an imbalance or an open range in
the market. A gap is incredibly strong
because it acts as a magnet for price.
So, if we've gapped up, very often the
market will come down to fill that gap.
Now, not every single time. Sometimes we
won't fill the gap or we'll fill the gap
much much later. But the majority of the
time these gaps are filled very quickly
which is why I say where the gap began
just before the close of the market.
Okay? So that this side of the gap acts
as a magnet for price. It's a very high
probability range for the market to run
back through which means we get easy
potential for trades. So, what we can
look to do is trade the market down

this is we only really have to look at
this bit of price action where the gap
has actually formed to find these
isolated positions. And if you can take
one of these trades on the day that the
market opens, so on a Monday, these gaps
generally form, you can be done for the
entire week off of a single gap trade.
